CURRENT AFFAIRS,

A gentleman by the name of Mr. James Little, we
learn for the first time in our lives, is Regius Professor
of Physic in the University of Dublin where the

* “famous’’ medical school is. The Regius Professor of
Physic to this world-renowned school has rushed into
print by way of explaining the exceptional branding
of herring 15 and herring 16.  His letter is not alto-
gether unamusing. Herring 16, who failed to carn a
brand in the clinical examination, it appears, “ was ono
of the most distinguished students who had been in the

~ University for several years, and had gained many

- prizes both during his arts course and in his medical
studies.”” Well, this throws a fresh gleam of light on
the Parochial University. If herring 15 was an extra

~ brilliant fish, what are we to think of the fish who are

- only sprats?

The Regius Professor has great faith in the credulity

of the shrewd Irish public. He says, “ and with refer-
nce to the inability to make the clinical examination to
‘which' the Mchbishon‘reféra, and which the Inspector

of the Medical Council had reported, I, at all eventt
was quite certain that the candidate failed, not becaus
he was not perfectly familiar with everything which h
should have done, but because he became so unnerve:
that he lost his presence of mind at the clinical examina
tion, which was held in the Mater Misericordizz Hos-
pital.”” Now we feel bound to say that though related
by the Regius Professor of Physic of Trinity of the
“famous ’’ medical school, we feel bound to say that
this strikes us as rather a tall story.  If “losing one’s
presence of mind’’ is to be accepted as explanation in
failures and other matters, the difficulties of examiners
and juries will be considerably increased.

On the evening of the disaster these two unbranded
herrings called upon the Regius Professor. He writes—
“No. 15 informed me that, never calculating on the
possibility of being rejected, he was under agreement
to take up the practice of a medical man in New Zea-
land, and was to sail in about two months. The other
candidate . did not make such a strong case, but it
scemed impossible to deal differently with the one and
the other. I told them that I had never known a re-
examination permitted, bufi that they might apply,
through their tutors, to the Board for the permission.”
To make a long story short, herrings 15 and 16 got
branded, anyway. The Board granted permission for
a re-examination of the rejected herrings, and herring
15 evidently kept his nerve and his presence of mind
the second time. The Regius Professor says:—“I1
should have taken steps to inform the Visitor of the
Medical Council of the re-examination, that if he
wished he might be present, and I should also have
called together a meeting of the Professors. I did not
do either, simply through inadvertence and the pressure
of other occupations. It did not occur to the Registrar
of the Medical School to do so, but simply on receiving
the findings of the Examiner in Mental Diseases he in-
cluded the two gentlemen in the list of passed men,
and when the day of Commencements came I presented
them for their Degrees.”’ It is, indeed, a very pretty
and perfectly-arranged story. In the first place you
see poor herring 15 simply, through loss of nerve and
presence of mind, utterly failing to do what in his
normal moments he could have easily done. Then he
asks for another trial, and “simply through inadvert-
ence and the pressure of other occupations,’” the Regius
Professor kept his mouth shut, and let out not even a
whisper to the Visitor of the Medical Council concern-
ing the second testing of the rejected herring; and
likewise “simply through inadvertence and the pres-
sure of other occupations’’ this absent-minded Regius
Professor neglected to call together a meeting of the
Professors concerning the second test of the rejected
herring who, on fhe first occasion, lost his presence of
mind. So much for the Regius Professor and the un-
nerved herring 15. By a happy coincidence the Re-
gistrar of thie Medical School is a sort of a lackadaisical,
absent-minded beggar also. It appears that “it did
not occur to the Registrar of the Medical School to do
50, but simply on receiving the findings of the Examiner
in Mental Diseasos he included the two gentlemen in
the list of passed men, and when the day of Commence-
ments came I presented them for their Degrees.”’ The
story of the Regius Professor is certainly well thought
out, each part fitting into the other \_vibh minute exact-
ness, Every unsophisticated and simple-minded per-
son in.the country, we feel sure, will open his eyes
with wonderment, and say that it was almost extra-
ordinary the manner in which everything fitted in so

nicely.

82 THE LEADER.

The Regius Professor of Physic goes on to relate how
he explained the whole matter to the Council in May,
1908. There was no adverse criticism. “On the con-
trary, one of the English members complimented me on
the clearness with which I had explained what had hap-
pened.”” We might remark that we have a great ap-
preciation of the sense of humour and the capacity for
satire of many Englishmen. It must have been quite
plain to all that the explanation of the Regius Pro-
fessor of Physic was “clear;” our own mild objection
to it would have been that it was just as wee little bit
too “ clear.””

The Regius Professor of Physic winds up his very
“ clear ”’ explanation with a paragraph that we can only
describe as a cross between a snarl and a very poor at-
tempt at a joke. He says:—“Tt will, however, I am
sure, be a gratification to the Archbishop to know that
No. 15, at all events, has not been let loose to endanger
the lives of any of his flock, but is now a prosperous
physician in New Zealand. About the ‘fate of No. 16
I have no information.” As an Irvishman that may be
some consolation to his Grace, but as a Catholic, he
would naturally be concerned for the lives of New Zea-
landers as well as for those of his own diocese.

We have not been very caroful students of Mr.
O’Brien’s controversy with the Irish Party, and we
did not read all the letters he published whilst in his
state of retirement. But we read several of his speeches
and we cannot say that we have received from them any
clear idea as to what he is really driving at. There is
evidently a row on; so much is quite plain. But for
our part we are yet as one on the outskirts of the crowd
who asks his neighbour “ What’s up ” What is it all
about? It certainly would be irony at the present
moment to style Mr. O’Brien the People’s William.

Mr. O’Brien is a powerful popular orator; we doubt
if he will find that his particular kind of oratorical
gifts will serve him very effectively on the unpopular
side. A harangue from Mr. O’Brien might inflame
people to an extreme course, and make them quite satis-
fied that what they wished to believe was true, but
his gifts appear to us to be somewhat out of place in a
campaign for “ moderation,”” Happily there is a great,
deal of good comedy in the business, and the resent-
ments of the public on either side must be watered
down with a large amount of laughter. Personally,
Mr. O’Brien is a man for whom we believe every Na-
tionalist has a kindly feeling. We have a kindly fecl-

ing for him ourselves, notwithstanding how we wmay

differ from him. As a sort of Mad Mullah denounczing

landlords at “magnificent ’’ meetings there was no ix
congruity, however one might regret his exaggerations
and indiscretions; but there is certainly something de-
cidedly incongruous in the spectacle of a characteristic
O’Brien campaign for—of all things in the world—
moderation. The O’Brien spectacles appear to have
too remarkable qualities: whilst looking through them
every friendly speck becomes magnified into something
magnificent ; every unfriendly thing becomes at least a
foul blot. What does Mr. O’Brien want? What is his
policy? If he has a moderate policy he at least ought
to moderate his language in saying so. There is a
thinking public in Ireland now, and a cool appeal to
thought will get somewhat of a fair hearing. But Mr.
O’Brien, we humbly think, is mistaken if he thinks
that it is possible to goad the people into conciliation.
For our part, as our readers well know, we are no
partisans of Mr. Dillon, Mr. Davitt or of the frecman’s
Jowrnal. If Mr, O’Brien has any case against these,
let him set it out clearly, coolly, and in argumentative
!anguagc, and he will receive a hearing from the think-
ing brainy section of the Irish people such as thoso
who read the Lraper. But thoughtful people will re-
fuse to hang any of these on the strength of heated ad-
jectives. Every friend: of Ireland feels humiliated and
depressed by these violent squabbles that divert energy
into worse than wasteful channels and gladden the
hearts of such “friends”” of Ireland as the Zrish Times.
; We fought for freedom of political criticism, and we
~welcome the reasonable expression of independent

So the British Government want to take away twenty-
two Parliamentary seats from Ireland. The proposal

_ will act as a tonic to political Ireland, and political

Ireland wants a tonic to pull it together just now.
The proposal is one on a par with pure, unabashed rob-
pory. The “Zrish’ Times with its characteristic
** Irish * spirit has not an editorial word to say to the
Government’s modest proposal in its issue of Tuesday.
The Arnotts and the imported editor are no doubt de-
lighted at the prospect of this contemplated robbery of
Ireland. It were about time that the Irish people com-
menced to hit out. This proposal hits the National
part of Ireland; it gives an additional member to Bel-
fast where the “Saved’’ bolt throwers and the “ civie
virtue ’’ come from. The teachers under the “ Na-
tional * Board are to be punished because some of them
refused to drink the toast that practically means “Damn
the Idolators.” We are to have mixed schools forced
upon us! “I do not believe,’”” said Cardinal Logue at
the Maynooth Union a few weeks ago, “certainly in
my memory, that the Government of the Country was
ever in such an unsatisfactory state, as far as Catholic
interests are concerned, as at the present moment.”’
He also said— “The country is being governed not
only without taking into consideration Catholic views,
or the views or interests of the Catholic majority, it is
being governed in open and candid opposition to them.”’
And now the Government, perhaps by way of conciliat-
ing us, proposes to take twenty-two Parliamentary
seats from Ireland. Mixed education, a Castle De-
partment to control primary education and other little
schemes, it may be calculated, will be all the easier
brought about when the Irish parliamentary representa-
tion is reduced by twenty-two, and when Belfast has an
additional member. The Catholics had better be look-
ing out for themselves if they do not want mixed
education and other things thrust upon them. It is
about time that as Catholics they looked to their de-
fences and prepared to assert their rights. We hear
a lot in England about the Nonconformist conscience ;
why does not the Catholic conscience of Ireland assert
itself, as it ought to assert itself, in Ireland as an or-
ganised entity.

Mr. M. Sullivan sent the following letter to the Sec-
retary of the Railway of which Mr. Frederick C. Pilk-
ington of the National Assurance Co. is one of the
Directors :—“Sir,—Acting on behalf of the Committee
of Shareholders, I beg to request that in case the direc-
tors solicit proxies for the next half-yearly meeting they
will first cause the half-yearly report to be issued so
that shareholders may have due time to consider the
information in the report before coming to a decision.
I beg to notify that should the dircctors apply for
proxies before the sharcholders have had an opportunity
of examining the half-yearly report, any attempt to
make use of such proxios will be contested. In con-
nection with the issuing of proxies I beg further to
protest against the use by any individual or individuals
of the company’s premises or staff.”’

. The fact that Mr. Sullivan had to make this protest
is a criticism in itself on the company of which Mr.
Frederick C. Pilkington, one of the financial wizards of
ﬂ}e National Assurance Co., is a director. Mr. Frede-
rick C. Pilkington and his colleagues may find the yield
of proxies heavier when harvested before the appear-
ance of the Report than after it ; be that as it may, it
is not fair to the shareholders like General Sir Henry
P. Ewart, K.C.B.; Commander E. Evans, R.N.; Colo-

‘nel W. Ofley, Lady Isabella Stewart, Major-General

Stokes, General Sir Alexander Taylor, G.C.B.; the
Hon. Albinia L. Brodrick, Rev. Riadore-Geldart, Rev.
Charles Turnerelli, and. others who are not satisfied
with the state of the company directed by Mr. Frederick
C. Pilkington and, his colleagues. The shareholders
should have an. opportunity, of reading the report befor'
they give a proxy in favour of the directors.

Mr. Sullivan’s protest: against the usiug‘ of any of
the' company’s staff or the company’s premises for the
purpose of gathering in proxies is very proper. ~We
do not see why Mr. Frederick C. Pilkington should not
occupy some of his spare time in addressing the enve-
lopes containing the requests to shareholders for their
proxies; and if, in consideration of a small fee, he got
permission from the National Assurance Co., of which
he is a distinguished director, to have the Great S. and
W. proxies in favour of him and his colleagues ad-
dressed fo him care of the Assurance Co., it would be a
very satisfactory arrangement, and the fee paid for the
accommodation would go towards reducing, in some
small way, the heavy call which unhappy widows,
orphans, and other distressed shareholders of that com-
pany are to be called upon to pay.

A branch of the Cork Industrial Development Asso-
ciation has been established in Kanturk. Mr. E. J.
O’Riordan, Secretary of the Cork Association, delivered
an address at which the Kanturk Branch was estab-
lished. He remarked :—‘ They might say it is very well
for you to ask us to support native industry, but are we
to sacrifice our hard-earned money to buy Irish goods
when we can buy imported goods much cheaper? He
said ‘No.” They were not asked to make any sacrifice
—they were merely asked to give preference to the home
product when it was as good value as that imported.
That was all his Association asked the Irish people to
do, and certainly no one could accuse them of com-
pelling them to strain their patriotism in doing so
much.”’

The Report of the Munster and Leinster Bank for
the half-year ending June 30th last is very satisfactory
reading. The shareholders are to get 12 per cent. in-
terest, £5,500 is to be transferred to Reserve, which
will then stand at £241,500—£2,500 is to be applied to
reduction of Premises Account, and it is proposed to carry
£6,174 2s. 6d. to next account. The net profits this
half-year amount to £20,174 9s. 3d. as against
£19,275 15s. 1d. for the corresponding period last year
—an increase of about £900. The net profit for the
corresponding  period in the year 1903  was
£18,887 1s. 4d. The sharcholders are certainly to be
congratulated on the prosperous and improving condi-
tion of their property, and they have every reason to
feel thankful to the staff of the bank. They will be
gratified to hear that the Trustees of the funds of May-
nooth College have recently transferred their account
to this bank. The Bank of Ireland, four of whose
directors have the honour of being amongst the finan-
cial wizards of the National Assurance Co., had had this
Maynooth account for about a century. It is pleasant
to find a popular bank with its head office in Cork com-
peting successfully with such a corporation as the Bank
of Ireland.

Cashel, July 8th, 1905.
DEAR SiR,—I read in the columns of the Dublin
dailies a few days since, that a bill promoted by the
bigoted directors of the above had passed through all
its stages in the House of Commons. Now, I say that
R 1o bill promoted by any Company should be allowed to
go through any one of its stages without the most
strenuous opposition on the part of the representatives
of Irish Catholic interests. What kind of old women
are we that we complain in one breath of their ill-
treatment of Catholics, and then fail to' direct our
M.P.’s to punish them where they have the power: to
do so. Twenty young men eagerly compete for the
pa.ltnest place going at Local Boards, and in face of
this what becomes of the public intelligence which al-
lows such free scope to companies which shut their doors
in the faces of young Catholics.—I am yours,
THOS. LAFFAN.

THE IRISH TOBACCO INDUSTRY.

"I‘HE attack by the British tobacco combine upon

the Irish tobacco industry is not only an interest-
ing, but a very important, study. There is bread and
butter in it as well as tobacco. Many thousands of
people would wear wry faces if they woke up one of these
fine mornings and found that the Irish tobacco industry
had vanished.

The Irish consumer has two great opportunities of
hitting back, and that with personal advantage to him-
self, in favour of the Irish tobacco industry. One op-
portunity is in connection with Irish roll tobacco. Every
purchaser of an ounce, or half an ounce, of Irish roll
should insist upon getting Irish roll for his money,
and should refuse to accept in exchange for the same
nmonoy bogus “ Irish roll *’ made in Britain. By adopt-
ing this policy, he pays no more; he gets the real and
not the bogus article; he helps to defeat an anti-Irish
trade game hatched in Britain, and he strengthens an
important and prosperous Irish industry. As a pre-
caution against having the spurious article foisted on
him he might moreover ask for the roll of some parti-
cular Trish manufacturer. There is no legal penalty
for selling ““ Irish roll ”” made in Britain, but it would
be another matter to palm it off as the roll of a speci-
fied Irish manufacturer. Irish roll is not branded
throughout, and therefore, if the purchaser would like
to see the maker’s brand on it, it would be necessary for
him to ask to be shown the entire roll—a request which
we presume in case of doubt would not be refused. In
the case of plug tobacco the maker’s brand appears
throughout and is perceptible on small purchases of two
ounces and soforth of this tobacco.  Another way
in which the popular Irish cigarette consumer can
strike an effective blow for Irish industry is by refusing
to buy any British penny packets of cigarettes. It
may appear a small, quite an insignificant, matter, not
counting much one way or the other whether the popu-
lar cigarette-smoker insists upon having a penny pack-
age of Irish-made cigarettes, or whether he consents
to accept from a behind-the-times tobacconist a penny-
worth: of British combine cigarettes. But, in the lump,
it is no small matter. Most of the principal Irish firms,
including Goodbody’s, Gallaher’s and Murray’s, manu-
facture the penny packet cigarette, and a member of a
great Irish tobacco firm assured us recently that if the
penny package of combine cigarettes were knocked out
of the Irish market, and its place taken by the Irish
penny package, that the change would provide employ-
ment for about 500 extra girls in Ireland at cigarette-
making. We have heard the opinion expressed that’
the combine penny-package cigarette is inferior to an
TIrish one. Every smoker. of cigarettes is an expert unto
himself. For the first time in our lives we smoked one
of the British penny-package cigarettes recently in order
to test it against a  Stdince Both packages contain
five cigarettes, and to outward appearance are similar
to one another. Our own verdict is that the latter is the
better. We put the cigarettes to another test. We
agked a smoker to smoke one of each without the
smoker at the time knowing which was which; the ver-
dict was docidedly in favour of the Sldinte cigarette.
In view of these tests wo cannot see why any Irish
cigarette-smoker—and more particularly in  face
of the combine’s onslaught on Irish roll—should buy a
package of combine cigaretites when better value can be
obtained of Irish make at the same money. The vari-
ous retail shops could do a lot in the way of helping an
Irish industry in this matter. An effort is worth mak-
ing in view of the estimate that it is possible on this
item alone to provide employment for an additional 500
girls in Ireland. ¢

. The bishops propose that the scholarships which they

; ha,y_e established should be open to women as well as to
men.  They have decided that scholarships awarded to
women are to be held in St. Mary’s University College,
and in Loreto College. With this arrangement no fault
need be found. But I hope that women will be put
upon a footing of real equality with men in respect of
University advantages. Giving them their share of the
proposed scholarshfps will not secure them equality in
respect of such advantages. Thoy ought, no matter
where they hold their scholarships, to be admitted to the
lectures given in University College by the Fellows of

- the Royal University.

It_ls impossible to find any adequate excuse for ex-
cludn}g them. As long as they are excluded they ave
suffering from a distinct grievance and injustice.
‘Women are admitted to Trinity College, to all its lec-
tures and degrees. Women are admitted into the Ceci-
lia Street School of Medicine. Does anyone believe
that women will, or can, be excluded from any future
University that is established? Things are now being
done which would not be thought of ten years ago.
‘Why persist at present in refusing to do what will be
done as the merest matter of course ten years hence—
especially when the refusal involves a distinct and un-
-equivocal injustice.

Women ask admission to those lectures not for the
mere fun of the thing, but for an abundantly sufficient
reason. They desire to avail themselves of what pre-
sumably is the best teaching to be had in Dublin for
Undergraduates of the Royal University; and, no mat-
ter where the Fellows lecture, this teaching, provided
at the public expense, ought, as a matter of simple jus-

tice, to be freely placed at the disposal of all
who wish to take advantage of it. They
also want to be present at the lectures given

by those who will examine them. The advantages of
‘the Royal University are placed equally at the disposal
of men and women. The greatest of those advantages
unquestionably is the teaching of the Fellows whom the
University pays. Why, then, are those advantages
closed to women in University College, which enjoys
gratis the service of the Catholic Fellows? Perhaps the
-appeal for scholarships may afford women an opportu-
aity of exacting redress of a grave injustice.

Before parting from this subject of scholarships, I
‘may say that it does not seem unreasonable to hope that
County and Urban Councils may see their way to estab-
lish scholarships to be awarded to brilliant pupils of
secondary schools within their administrative areas,
especially pupils of restricted means, to aid them in
obtaining University education. If they have not power
at present to appropriate money for this purpose, it is
to be hoped that steps will be taken to have them in-
vested with such power.

2. It is not enough to make a practical effort to put
-such, University education as we possess within reach of
‘the largest possible number. We require to extend
.and perfect our educational equipment. At present
‘what have we?- University College for men, St. Mary’s
University College and Loreto College (two purely pri-
vate establishments) for women, and the Cecilia Street
:School of Medicine. We need not take further account
of the two women’s Colleges. University College and
the Medical School are public institutions, although
the former has been Tor several years under private
‘management. j

Both schools require development. They are insuffi-
.ciently housed. They have not sufficient appliances

_even on the most modest scale. They have not enough
professors or tutors, and those that they have (even those
who enjoy Royal University Fellowships) are insuffi-
ciently paid. We want, in addition, a school of Irish

" studies under national control and diroction ; we should

obviously have the best School of Irish Studies in the
‘world. We want a School of Law. We want a School
.of Engineering and Applied Science. We want Labora-

The first thing to be done is to put University College
on a proper footing. The domestic administration and
discipline might for the present be allowed to remain as
thoy are. Reform of academic administration is ur-
gently nceded. There is at present an Academic Council,
but, according to some of its members, it has little orno
power; its sole function seoms to be to advise the Presi-
dent, who may, or may not, be guided by its decisions.
Tt should, in academic matters, be vested with full
power. It should appoint new professors. It should
recommend to the Senate of the Royal University in
case of appointment to Fellowships to be held by Catho-
lics. It is outragdous that all Catholic Fellows should
practically be appointed by a single member of the
Royal University Senate. Such a system is anomalous
and, under any circumstances, would be indefensible ;
in the absence of confidence in its actual working, it is
worse than indefensible.

The Medical School, as regards academic administra-
tion, seems to be Fairly sabisfactory.

3. Passing from the domestic and academic adminis-
tration- of the existing Colleges, if we are to aim at
development and expansion, we require a Board which
will exercise a general control over both the existing
Colleges and any Colleges or schools of University status
that may hereafter be established. The appointment of
such a Board, thoroughly representative of Nationalist
TIreland, should be one of the principal concerns of a
National Conference on the University Question. To it
would be entrusted thoe administration of the finances
of the proposed University. It should contain the best
elements—lay and clerical—of Nationalist Ireland. It
should be so constituted that the public and all intend-
ing benefactors of the University should have absolute
confidence in its business and financial capacity. It
should be incorporated ; and it should interfere as little
as possible with the academic governing bodies of the
various Colleges and schools in purely academic matters.

4. Suppose we have such a Board, the next question
is to find funds to provide for efficiency, development,
and expansion. It seems to me that we ought not to
go to work on the lines of endeavouring to supply our
needs by means of an annual collection. We ought to
aim at providing a capital sum, as large as we can get.
All moneys obtained should be invested, and as the re-
venue from the capital sum grows, we should endeavour
by its means to perfect and expand our University
system. New chairs would be founded from time to
time as the funds for their endowment would become
available. Occasionally a new School or department,
or a new Laboratory or Museum, would, under similar
conditions, begin in a small way, and afterwards de-
velop. A beginning of a School of Irish Studies should
be made as soon as possible. Every forward step should
be taken with care. Steady and secure progress, not
brilliant venGures, should characterise the enterprise.

If we have a Board to which money can be entrusted,
a good deal of money will be forthcoming. The Na-
tional Conference, having found its Board, should make
an appeal for funds to the Irish race. Ten or twelve
collectors—lay and clerical—should be sent amongst the
Irish  abroad. The Irish-American  millionaires
should get a chance of emulating the munificence of Mr.
Carnegie in reference to the Universities of his native
land. Collections_should be arranged for at home, and
subscriptions and donations invited, but every penny
obtained should go to the capital fund. New chairs, as
ostablished, should be separately and permanently en-
dowed. If we go to work in this way, we shall be doing
something practical.  Every year will show distinct
and important progress, and in ten years we shall have
gone a long way towards solving the University ques-
tion.

AGRICULTURAL PRICES AND IRELAND.

A GRICULTURAL Treland may suffer severely
V) from ecither of two facts—an increased output of
certain agricultural produce in British colonigs
and foreign countries would tend to pull down prices in
the market; a diminution of British demand would
have a like effect; both combined would aggravate the
case, and the lowering of the prices for foreign cattle,
sheep and dairy produce in the British market in exist-
ing circumstances reacts with damaging effect upon the
agriculturalists of Ireland. A weak and beaten Eng-
land has its own attractions for the Irish mind; but
the more meat and butter England is rich enough to
cat the better for the Irish farmer as things go in the
world these times. The United States is the home of
an unascertained number of millions of the Irish race;
but, notwithstanding that fact, if the United States
were so weakened and crippled that it could send no
beef to the British markets (they sent, on an average, the
equivalent of 828,995 cattle to the British markets
every year during the years 1899-1903), that fact,
though it might hurt the prospects of some of the Irish
race abroad, would tend considerably to raise prices in
Ireland for what some of the Irish race at home have to
export. There are some patriotic Irishmen in Argen-
tina; however, during the quinquennial period, 1899-
1903, Argentina sent the equivalent of an annual aver-
age of about 2,675,168 sheep to the British markets,
whilst Ireland only found a market in Britain of an
annual average during the same period of about 891,804.
If Argentina exported no sheep, this country would be
considerably the richer in consequence of the inevitable
rise in the price of Irish mutton.

Agricultural Treland—and the Irish farmer is the pro-
ducer in this country—whether it likes it or not, is
driven into the position of challenging the world. The
farmer, though he has paid twenty or twenty-four years’
purchase for his land, has to step out with as bold a face
as he can, and say—“Come, the four quarters of the
world, in arms.”” For he, and he in the main is Ire-
land, has the  privilege ’’ of being part of the British
Empire, and Britain, the centre of that Empire,
naturally wants to buy its food in the cheapest market,
and so Erinshire has to face the game, and make shift
to play it as best it can according to the rules. The
most consoling factor for one who attempts to think out
the situation is that the future is uncertain; but the
probabilities are calculated to breed pessimists rather
than optimists.

We have just finished an interesting pamphlet * that
tackles the question in an able manner. My, M‘Car-
thy’s pamphlet is well worth reading, and we would be
pleased to know that it was widely read. It would be
well for Ireland if large numbers of TIrish minds
grappled with the problem that is the subject of M.
M‘Carthy’s inquiry. It would, at least, free many en-
thusiastic people from many a foolish notion.  Mr.
M‘Carthy’s inquiry covers some unknown and some un-
cerbain quantitics; bub the attempt to think out the
question is a masculine one, and it is calculated to in-
spire a liko speculative turn in the reader’s mind.

Whilst the do-nothing West British landlords, who
have been the great curse of Ireland, want bheir, legal

- matters compared with the unknown determining quan-
til {es—tha world movements in supply and demand
which have power to send up or down the Irish farmer
as if he was a cork on the waves.

If England wcub_ down her demand for agricultural
produce .\vo"ld shrink, and that would tend to pull
down pricei {so. The economic tondency that may re-

- duce England as a manufacturing country reacts on the
fortunes of Irish agriculture. If—and in all specula-
tions as to the cconomic future “if ’* is necessarily a
big factor—if Britain went down, and the supply of
produce from Argentina, Russia, Australia, New Zea-
land, and other places expanded considerably, what
would become of Ireland? ~ On a broken market how
much extra produce would Ireland have to export in
order to realise the wherewithal to pay its yearly land
purchase instalment? How much more would sho have
to export in exchange for the manufactured articles
she imported? A careful perusal of Mr. Callaghan
M‘Carthy’s essay is calculated to steady the reflective
Irish mind.

A possible factor in the future not. touched upon at
all by Mr. M‘Carthy is the rise of an industrial Ireland.
At present Ireland has to realise agricultural produce
in the world market in order to meet the bill for her
manufactured imports. A development in the way of
reducing the volume of that exchange by enabling Irish
agriculturalists to exchange Irish produce for Irish-
manufactured goods is a possibility of the future. Mr.
M‘Carthy does not refer to it, whether from oversight
or because he has little hopes of such a development,
we cannot say. Yet the situation which the contempla-
tion of the possible course of the agricultural world
markets presents to the mind, impresses one forcibly with
the necessity of a development on the manufacturing
side in Ireland. From a national point of view the
fall in the prices of Irish agricultural produce chiefly
counts in view of the fact that so much of it has to be
realised in the British markets. Development in the
way of small farmers producing their own food—instead
of living so much on American bacon, foreign flour and
tea—would render them less dependent on market prices.
What is it to a family who live on potatoes, milk, and
meat produced on their own holding what the prices of
these commodities are in the market? Very little com-
pared with what they would be to them if they depended
for their food on the exchange value of these commodi-
ties. Two main things for Ireland to aim at are:—(1)
‘To live as far as possible on its own produce; (2) to
exchange its own produce as far as possible for its own
manufactured articles.

So long as the land purchase instalments have to be
paid, the farmers will have to realise produce to their

- imports what it ought reasonably to produce itself in the
way of manufactured goods, so long will it have to
‘realise an undue share of its produce in the British
- narket. Speculations for the future are intensely in-
teresting though necessarily qualified by many “ifs.”’
Mz. Callaghan M‘Carthy has written an interesting and

and how it is managed, I will let Mr. Howley tell.*

“The Senior Fellows of Trinity College are not the
proprietors, but only the stewards and trustees of the
public endowment ; and it follows that if those stewards
are found to have managed the national property un-
faithfully, they may justly be deprived of their steward-
ship, and more responsible persons may be appointed in
their stead. The Commission of 1853 reported that
the estates of Trinity College comprised over 200,000
acres of land, valued at £92,000 a year; but that the
actual rental and renewal fines only produced £34,000
per annum. The value of the property ought now to
be near £108,000; but it seems the receipts have only
reached £37,000. Mark well, every friend of integrity,
that the difference between the value and the rental of
‘th lands has arisen from the mismanagement, and I
‘believe I must say, the misappropriation of the Senior
Fellows. The Report of 1853 admits ‘that leases have
been usually granted on payment of fines, at a rent con-
siderably below the true value.” Why? Is there any
clue to this fraud on the public endowment? Let the
Report speak, and give the reason for this masked
peculation.  ‘Because,” says the Report, the fines
were distributed amongst the Provost and Senior Fel-
lows, as part of their respective incomes.” And twenty
years ago, Parliament interfered, and a Royal letter
‘bought off those customary delinquents, the Provost and
Senior Fellows, by allowing them to ‘receive £800 a
year each, out of the general funds of the College, in
lieu of their share of the fines.” The contrast between
‘the value and the rental of the College -estates makes
manifest to what an extent the Fellows neglected their

latest apologist for Trinity College, is candid enough to
admit that there are “ some useless offices which should
be abolished.” I shall arraign and condemn
Trinity College, out of the mouth of her own spokes-
man. We are informed by Mr. Mahafiy, in the Fort-
nightly Review, that the thirty-three Fellows of Trinity
College are not voracious when they take £27,000, in
the shape of students’ fees, as this large sum represents
the carnings of the Fellows by teaching ; but the £27,000
not being enough to pay them for managing as well as
teaching, they, in addition, help themselves to £11,800
out of the annual revenue of the College. And yet, the
Fellows who have thus divided amongst themselves
nearly £39.000 a year for the duties of teaching and
managing, find it necessary to employ Professors at a
cost of £6,000 to aid them to do one of the duties, for
which they have already paid themselves so handsomely.
« . . Tt seems to me a great scandal, and a violation
of decency and common sense that the Fellows should
pay themselves out of a public endowment, at their own
valuation of their services; and it seems to me a greater
scandal, if possible, that they should drive a roaring
trade in Examinations. They appropriate to themselves
an enormous income derived from the fees of students;
and thus it happens that they have a strong interest in
not making examinations too difficult—the rugged
heights of Parnassus are made smooth that the climbing
students may pay their fees with alacrity and patience.
The passing of examinations has too near an approach to
the reality of a money bargain. ‘When even
they, who have been nurtured at Trinity College, who
have shared its emoluments, and gained its honours, re-
volt at the system of distributing the College funds; are
those Irishmen who are practically excluded from the
University to assume an air of content and resignation?
I could speak of several agitations among the scholars
to obtain increased rewards for talent; but discontent
has still reached a higher sphere, and the power of the
Board has been wielded to put down incipient mutiny
among the Junior Fellows. An unjust monopoly fears
exposure, trembles at publicity, and welcomes only a
mellifluous commission or a friendly visitation. The
Board in 1858 censured and silenced two of the Junior
Feollows, who sought to expose the abuses of the College
in the public Press; and the censure upon one of the
Fellows was confirmed on an appeal to the visitation
body of the University. It seems that the condemned
Junior Fellow was guilty of a breach of discipline, when
he publicly alleged that the Board distributed the re-
vonues of ‘the College in a manner opposed to the in-
terests of education ; and he also appears not to have re-
flected that once a use is ever a custom—and that the
Board, by long enjoying fees, acquire a right to enjoy
them ; but the merits of a technical dispute waged before
a domestic tribunal are not the merits of a public ques-
tion, which appeals to the entire country for sympathy
and support, and to Parliament for redress and justice.
The Junior Fellows have been silenced in their com-
plaints; but the Catholics of Treland shall not wear a
padlock on their lips. Let not the Senior Fellows dream
they have for ever silenced discord in the spheres; there

THE LEADER.

- are thunder-clouds drifting towards them, and when the

‘orm has burst their monopoly shall be swept away,
and the genius of free education shall stand unstained
by condoned corruption, unswayed by religious ran-
cour.’’

Our .only experience of Catholic Universities in Ire-
land has not been, it is true, an experience of succoss;
once in the 17th century, and again in the 19th. No
wonder these attempts more or less failed; the most
lasting purpose they could in their circumstances have
served was a national protest against educational wrong.
Our circumstances have brought us to associate Protes-
tanism or secularism with higher education as ideas
which naturally blend, and, as if against our instincts,
to consider Catholic faith and higher education incom-
patible. It is one of the remaining relics of our past
disabilities ; marks of slavery which are not yot quite
healed, though the chains which made them have been
cast off. We have kept the faith; we glory in the
sacrifices it has cost, and we would, if necessary, make
them all over again for its sake; and yet we hesitato to
claim for it a foremost place in the movement of
natural enlightenment. Our religious instinct recoils
from non-Catholicism under any form, from High
Church to No Church, and yet it is associated in our
thoughts with educational and social superiority. It
is a feeling which must have an injurious influence on
us, since it connects intellectual stagnation with religious
truth, and what is progressive in reason with what is
false in faith. For the impression itself we rather de-
serve praise, since it is a record of sacrifice for prin-
ciple and conscience; the blame lies with those who
have withheld from us the opportunity and the right
of witnessing in our midst the influence and the work
of a Catholic University duly chartered and sufficiently
endowed. The Catholic University founded in Dublin
by the Irish Bishops in 1854 did not realise their hopes,
nor yet has it been quite a failure. I cannot paint
the light and the shadow better than in this lengthy
passage which I borrow from a Lecture delivered before
the Catholic Graduates’ Association, on the 10th of last
February by Dr. Sigerson, F.R.U.I., who spoke from
personal knowledge :—“ The method of carrying out the
project was such and so wise that, had it obtained due
recognition, it would have placed their (the bishops’)
conduct above all carping. It has been constantly al-
leged that the aim of the Irish bishops was to make the
University an engine of clerical domination over the
laity. If they had desired that they need never have
striven as champions of lay education. What was their
course upon the present occasion? They went outside
their rank, and even outside their country to seek for
and gain the one most capable man perhaps in the
world as a University head, Dr. John Henry Newman.
They undertook the responsibilities of financing the
Institution; they gave him charge to draw up a Con-
stitution. A prompt and generous response met their
appeal for funds, over £58,000 being contributed in five
years, of which sum one-fourth came from the exiled
Irish in the United States and elsewhere.* From the

“out of all the Dublin Hospitals, only three had any
Catholic practitioner in them at all. On the
other hand, out of sixty-two medical officers altogether
in the various hospitals, the Catholic portion did not
exceed the number of ten. Again, out of five Medical
Schools in Dublin® (exclusive of the University) three
had no Catholic lecturer at all, and the other two one
a-piece; so that out of 49 lecturers only two were Catho-
lic. Putting the two lists together, we find that out of
111 medical practitioners in situations of trust and
authority, 12 were Catholic and 99 were Protestant.’”’
Those who can now look around them, and see their
Medical School occupying the foremost position in the
city, with great hospitals in co-operation, should remem-
ber that without the impulse of the University there
might have been no such changes, and that without such
or a similar centre of intellectual energy there is sure
to be decadence and loss of status. It may be asked,
why did not the other Faculties succeed as well as that
of Medicine? The answer is that the Medical Faculty
had but to ask for recognition of its lectures from the
College of Surgeons and Physicians, and these, with
that enlightened and large-minded liberality which has
always been' the characteristic of a progressive and
humane profession, immediately gave their sanction.
The other Faculties had to appeal to the Governments
of the time, and though they shared some of the most
distinguished Professors of the period, working under a
most famous Rector, their plea was rejected. It cer-

a diploma was at once granted to a proficient student,

University memorialised Earl Russell in favour of the

Senate accepted the Charter by a majority, but the
Belfast graduates, a majority in convocation, rejected

understood at the time that had the Charter proved
